description: ML prediction datasets for two OpenMuscle sensor devices — OM-12 (SensorBand) and FlexGrid-64. Each dataset contains raw sensor readings, ground-truth LASK5 labels, and model predictions on holdout data never seen during training.

devices.name: OM-12 SensorBand
devices.description: 3 SensorBand banks (OM-SB-V1-C.0/1/2), each with 4 hall-effect sensors. Legacy wearable form factor.

training.note: Raw .txt captures converted via legacy packet parser with timestamp-based pairing (±100ms window)

devices.name: FlexGrid-64
devices.description: 4x16 hall-effect pressure sensor grid. Higher resolution successor to OM-12. Captures full hand pressure distribution.

training.note: ExtraTrees selected after comparing 6 model types on same split. All data from same capture session — cross-session models fail due to sensor baseline drift.

methodology.overview: Both devices use hall-effect sensors to measure finger pressure/flexion. The LASK5 linear actuator system provides ground-truth finger position labels (4 channels, normalized 0-1). Models are trained to predict LASK5 labels from sensor readings alone.
pipeline: 1. Data capture: sensor packets and LASK5 labels are received over UDP and paired by timestamp
2. CSV export: paired data is saved with sensor columns (R{row}C{col} or Sensor_{n}) and label columns (label_0 through label_3)
3. Train/test split: holdout data is kept separate and never seen during training
4. Model training: tree-based regressors (RandomForest or ExtraTrees) trained on sensor→label mapping
5. Inference: trained model predicts labels from holdout sensor data
6. Export: sensor data, true labels, and predictions exported as JSON for web visualization
key_findings: FlexGrid-64 (R²=0.660) significantly outperforms OM-12 (R²=0.264) due to 5x more sensors capturing finer pressure gradients
ExtraTrees outperforms RandomForest by ~5% R² on FlexGrid data — random split thresholds reduce overfitting on noisy sensor readings
Sensor baselines drift between capture sessions, making cross-session prediction unreliable without calibration
More same-session training data is the single biggest accuracy driver (0.330 → 0.660 by adding first-half of test file to training)
Tree count has diminishing returns — 100 vs 500 trees gives <0.002 R² improvement
label_3 is consistently hardest to predict across both devices, suggesting less distinct sensor activation for that finger

data_format.sensor_data: Object with sensor column names as keys, each containing an array of float values per sample
data_format.labels: Object with label_0 through label_3 as keys, each containing ground-truth float arrays (0-1)
data_format.predictions: Object with label_0 through label_3 as keys, each containing predicted float arrays
data_format.metadata: Device info, sample counts, train/test split details, R² metrics

files.om12_capture_72_predictions.json: OM-12 sensor data + labels + predictions (1480 samples)
files.flexgrid64_holdout_predictions.json: FlexGrid-64 sensor data + labels + predictions (683 samples)
files.flexgrid64_viewer.html: Interactive HTML viewer for FlexGrid-64 (requires serving via HTTP)
files.model_comparison.json: Raw R² scores for all 6 models tested on FlexGrid-64
